summary: Deleted the leftover assembly file asm/MSL/cstring.s after the corresponding MSL cstring routines had been matched in C elsewhere. The removed assembly contained implementations/symbols such as func_8032388C, memcmp, memchr, and memmove, plus calls to copy helper routines like __copy_longs_unaligned, __copy_longs_rev_unaligned, __copy_longs_aligned, and __copy_longs_rev_aligned. No PR body or review discussion was present in the slice, so the evidence is limited to the title and single-file deletion diff.

key_files.notes: Removed 132 lines of handwritten/placeholder assembly for matched MSL cstring routines.

what_changed: Removed assembly definitions for func_8032388C, memcmp, memchr, and memmove from the asm fallback tree.
Removed local branch labels and low-level loop code associated with the cstring assembly implementation.
The PR title states the reason directly: cstring.s was removed because it was matched.
smart_moves: Removed obsolete assembly once the corresponding code was matched, reducing duplicate sources of truth.
Kept the PR narrowly scoped to a single cleanup file deletion, making the match-cleanup intent easy to verify.
Targeted an MSL library assembly file rather than mixing the cleanup with unrelated decomp changes.
decomp_lessons: After matching a source file, remove its asm fallback so the build and repository reflect the decompiled implementation as authoritative.
Small cleanup PRs that delete matched asm files are useful milestones in decomp progress and make remaining unmatched assembly easier to audit.
MSL/library code can include standard symbols like memcmp, memchr, and memmove; once matched, their old assembly stubs should not linger in asm/MSL.
When a PR only removes an asm file, preserve confidence by relying on the title and diff; without review comments or body text, avoid inferring the exact C replacement.
naming_conventions: MSL assembly fallbacks were organized under asm/MSL/ with library-style file names such as cstring.s.
Standard C library symbols in the deleted assembly used canonical names such as memcmp, memchr, and memmove.
Unidentified or less-clear matched symbols could still appear as address-derived names such as func_8032388C before full naming cleanup.
Local assembly labels used address-derived lbl_803xxxxx names.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: The deleted memmove assembly selected aligned versus unaligned and forward versus reverse copy helper paths, referencing __copy_longs_unaligned, __copy_longs_rev_unaligned, __copy_longs_aligned, and __copy_longs_rev_aligned.
The deleted memcmp and memchr implementations used byte-load update loops with addic. countdown patterns and early returns.
The cleanup indicates the matching tactic had advanced far enough that this entire asm/MSL/cstring.s fallback was no longer needed.
No exact C source file or matching technique is shown in the slice, so the replacement implementation cannot be identified from this PR context alone.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or reviews were present in the provided PR slice.
No explicit reviewer feedback or requested changes were available.
follow_up_queries: Find the C source file that matched asm/MSL/cstring.s before PR 184.
Search for current definitions of memcmp, memchr, memmove, and func_8032388C in the MSL decomp source.
Audit remaining asm/MSL files for matched-library cleanup opportunities.
Check build configuration or linker order changes around the time cstring.s was removed.
